content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 14 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 107v–107r | **Source Images:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/DEU_014_page_001.jpg, DEU_014_page_002.jpg | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 152–153

---

1 You are sons of the LORD your God. You shall not cut yourselves or make any baldness between your eyes for the dead.

2 For you are a people holy to the LORD your God, and the LORD has chosen you to be a people for his own treasured possession, out of all the peoples who are on the face of the earth.

3 You shall not eat any abomination.

4 These are the animals you may eat: the ox, the sheep, the goat,

5 the deer, the gazelle, the roe deer, the wild goat, the ibex, the antelope, and the mountain sheep.

6 Every animal that parts the hoof and has the hoof cloven in two and chews the cud, among the animals, you may eat.

7 Yet of those that chew the cud or have the hoof cloven, you shall not eat these: the camel, the hare, and the rock badger, because they chew the cud but do not part the hoof — they are unclean to you.

8 And the pig, because it parts the hoof but does not chew the cud — it is unclean to you. Their flesh you shall not eat, and their carcasses you shall not touch.

9 Of all that are in the waters you may eat these: whatever has fins and scales you may eat.

10 And whatever does not have fins and scales you shall not eat; it is unclean to you.

11 You may eat all clean birds.

12 But these are the ones from which you shall not eat: the eagle, the bearded vulture, the black vulture,

13 the red kite, the falcon, and the kite of any kind;

14 every raven of any kind;

15 the ostrich, the nighthawk, the sea gull, and the hawk of any kind;

16 the little owl, the great owl, the barn owl,

17 the pelican, the vulture, the cormorant,

18 the stork, the heron of any kind, the hoopoe, and the bat.

19 And all winged insects are unclean to you; they shall not be eaten.

20 All clean winged creatures you may eat.

21 You shall not eat anything that has died a natural death. You may give it to the sojourner who is within your gates, that he may eat it, or you may sell it to a foreigner. For you are a people holy to the LORD your God. You shall not boil a young goat in its mother's milk.

22 You shall tithe all the yield of your seed that comes from the field year by year.

23 And you shall eat before the LORD your God, in the place that he will choose to make his name dwell there, the tithe of your grain, of your new wine, and of your oil, and the firstborn of your herd and flock, so that you may learn to fear the LORD your God always.

24 And if the way is too long for you, so that you are not able to carry the tithe, when the LORD your God blesses you, because the place that the LORD your God will choose to put his name there is too far from you,

25 then you shall turn it into money and bind up the money in your hand and go to the place that the LORD your God chooses.

26 And you shall spend the money for whatever you desire — for oxen or sheep or wine or strong drink, whatever your appetite craves. And you shall eat there before the LORD your God and rejoice, you and your household.

27 And you shall not neglect the Levite who is within your gates, for he has no portion or inheritance with you.

28 At the end of every three years you shall bring out all the tithe of your produce in the same year and lay it up within your gates.

29 And the Levite — because he has no portion or inheritance with you — and the sojourner, the fatherless, and the widow, who are within your gates, shall come and eat and be satisfied, so that the LORD your God may bless you in all the work of your hand that you do.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

**Verse 1 — banim atem la-YHWH Eloheikhem (you are sons of the LORD your God):** This remarkable filial address grounds the holiness commands that follow: Israel's identity as God's children motivates purity. LXX: *huioi este kyriou tou theou hymon* — exact parallel.

**Verse 1 — lo titgodedu / lo tasimu qorchah (cutting / baldness):** "Cut yourselves" for *titgodedu* (Hithpael of *gadad*, to gash) and "make any baldness between your eyes" for *taqrichu* (Hiphil of *qarach*, to make bald). These were mourning rites associated with pagan practice. LXX: *ou katatemneisthe* and *ou poiesete phalagrothran*.

**Verse 2 — am segullah (treasured possession):** "Treasured possession" for *segullah* — a term for a personal treasure or special property. The same word occurs at Exod 19:5. LXX: *laon periousion* ("a people around/about" — the chosen possession).

**Verses 4–5 — animal names:** The Hebrew animal vocabulary is notoriously difficult. LOGOS renders: *tzvi* = gazelle, *yachmur* = roe deer, *acko* = wild goat, *dishon* = ibex, *teo* = antelope, *zemer* = mountain sheep. These follow standard scholarly identifications while acknowledging uncertainty. LXX uses a partly different identification scheme.

**Verse 7 — arnevet / shafan (hare / rock badger):** The *arnevet* is the hare (or rabbit); the *shafan* is the hyrax/rock badger — not a true ruminant, but exhibits jaw movements resembling cud-chewing. "Rock badger" follows the most defensible identification. LXX: *dasypoda* (hare) and *chirogrylion* (hyrax).

**Verse 21 — nebeilah (carcass / died natural death):** "Anything that has died a natural death" for *nebeilah* (lit. that which fell — an animal that died without proper slaughter). Given to the *ger* (sojourner) or sold to a *nokri* (foreigner); the distinction in social categories is significant. LXX: *ptoma* — a fallen/dead thing.

**Verse 21 — lo tevashel gedi (not boil a kid):** The prohibition against boiling a young goat in its mother's milk appears three times in the Pentateuch (Exod 23:19; 34:26; Deut 14:21). Placed here at the end of the dietary section, it may form a climactic or boundary marker. LXX: *ou hepseseis arnion en galakti metros autou* — with *arnion* (lamb) rather than *gedi* (kid), a divergence from MT.

**Verse 22 — ma'aser (tithe):** "Tithe" consistently for *ma'aser*. The second tithe of Deuteronomy (vv. 22–26) is distinct from the Levitical tithe (Num 18) — it was brought to Jerusalem and eaten there by the worshipper. LXX renders throughout with *dekate* (tenth).

**Verse 26 — nefesh (appetite / soul):** "Whatever your appetite craves" for *kol asher tish'alekha nafshekha* (lit. "whatever your soul asks of you"). The word *nefesh* here carries the sense of desire, appetite, life-force. Not translated "soul" in the abstract metaphysical sense. LXX: *ean epithyme he psyche sou* — "if your soul desires."

**Verse 29 — ha-ger, ha-yatom, ve-ha-almanah (the sojourner, the fatherless, and the widow):** The triadic formula for the vulnerable classes in Deuteronomic law. The Levite is added to make four groups without land-based income. This social legislation is distinctive of Deuteronomy's covenantal concern for the marginalized.

*— LOGOS*
